projects.title: Toobmap (WIP)
projects.tagline: Mapping the journey of travel videos.
projects.description: An exploration into synchronizing geographical data with video playback. Developing both a browser extension for quick context and a standalone web app for deep exploration.

projects.title: MIDI Key Finder
projects.description: A tool for identifying musical scales and keys from MIDI files or live input, using note emphasis as a heuristic for detection.

blogPosts.title: Building a map for YouTube videos

video.caption: Current progress: Synchronized 3D map following the video's narrative.

blogPosts.intro: Toobmap started as a personal need to understand the geography of the travel vlogs I watch. As the project evolved, I realized that it had the potential to be a useful tool for geographic exploration and learning.

content.body: I often watch travel videos from places I didn't even know existed. Suddenly, I find myself switching back and forth between YouTube and Google Maps, trying to keep up. I love understanding where we are in the world, but creators often skip the geography. So I thought: Why not show the map directly on YouTube?

While developing the project, I've been oscillating between two different implementations. The first is a Chrome Extension, designed for quick, in-the-moment context. It lives as a lightweight overlay directly inside the YouTube interface, perfect for a quick lookup when you wonder 'where exactly is this dune in Namibia?' without leaving the flow of the video.

The second is a standalone Web App. This is where I can experiment more with the 3D aspect of the map. By having more screen real estate, I can provide a full-size rotatable map that synchronizes with the video playback, making it more suited for long-form exploration and geographic learning.

Automatically identifying places in video text sounded simple at first. I started with NLP (Natural Language Processing) to find potential place names and matched them against a local geographical database. But context is critical—algorithmically distinguishing between 'New York' the city and 'New York' in a random description is hard.

The breakthrough came with AI. Now, the data is sent to a lightweight language model (Gemma) that understands linguistic context. It catches nuances I could never write manual rules for. The map updates with the model's response within seconds.

The extension is currently built with TypeScript, Svelte 5, and Leaflet. As I continue to develop both versions, the goal is to make them work seamlessly together, providing geography as a first-class citizen in the video-watching experience.
